title: Positioning Critical Information
concept: Deliberately placing the most important facts near the start or end of the context to counteract attention bias.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: what did we learn about 'distractor documents' in the last lesson?

options.text: Irrelevant but topically similar text in the context can measurably lower answer accuracy
Adding more documents always improves accuracy no matter what they contain
Distractor documents only affect image models, not text models

exercises.explain: Right — distractor documents look relevant on the surface but don't actually help answer the question, and their presence can confuse the model and hurt accuracy, even when the real answer is also present.

cards.title: The Sandwich Trick
cards.body: Ever notice you remember the first and last things on a grocery list way better than the middle? LLMs have the same quirk — so smart prompt builders put the must-know facts at the start or end, like the bread of a sandwich.

cards.title: What Is Positioning?
cards.body: Positioning critical information means intentionally placing the most important facts near the beginning or the very end of your prompt/context, since those are the spots models pay the most attention to. It's a practical workaround, not a fix for the underlying attention bias.

cards.title: Front Page News
cards.body: Newspaper editors put the biggest story on the front page and the punchiest line at the very end of the article, not buried in paragraph 12. LLMs read context the same way readers skim a paper — they notice the headline and the closer, and their eyes glaze over in the middle.

cards.title: Before vs After
cards.body: Before: A 3000-word contract is pasted, then at word 3001 you ask 'What's the termination clause?' — buried mid-document, it's easy to miss. After: You restate the exact termination clause right before your question at the end, so the model sees it in the high-attention zone right when it needs to answer.

cards.title: Myth: Order Doesn't Matter
cards.body: Myth: 'As long as the fact is somewhere in the context, the model will find it.' Truth: research shows accuracy can drop noticeably when key facts sit in the middle versus the edges — even though the model technically 'read' every word.

cards.title: Attention Across the Context
cards.body: Picture attention like a U-shaped curve across your context: high at the start, dips low in the middle, rises again at the end. Smart placement rides that curve instead of fighting it.

left.text: Key detail placed in the middle → often missed or underweighted

right.text: Same detail moved to the start or end → reliably picked up

exercises.prompt: You have a 10-page research report and need the model to accurately use one key statistic buried on page 6. What's the best fix using positioning?

options.text: Leave it on page 6 but bold the text
Repeat the key statistic again near the end of the context, right before your question
Delete pages 1-5 so page 6 becomes the start
Translate the statistic into a different language so it stands out

exercises.explain: Repeating the crucial statistic near the end (right before the question) puts it in a high-attention zone, dramatically increasing the odds the model uses it correctly — without needing to restructure the whole document.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Placing critical information at the start or end of context guarantees the model will always answer correctly.
exercises.explain: Positioning improves the odds by working around attention bias, but it's not a guarantee — other factors like distractors, ambiguity, or model limitations can still cause mistakes.

exercises.sentence: Because of attention bias, it's smart to place the most important fact near the start or the end of the context.
exercises.explain: Start and end positions get the most model attention, so critical facts belong there — not buried in the middle.

exercises.prompt: Which explanation of 'positioning critical information' was most likely written by an AI and sounds generic/padded rather than concrete?

options.text: Positioning critical information is a multifaceted strategic paradigm that leverages optimal placement methodologies to enhance overall contextual comprehension outcomes.
Put your key fact near the start or end of the prompt — models pay less attention to the middle, so that's where things get missed.
If the answer depends on one fact, repeat that fact right before you ask your question — it boosts the odds the model uses it.

exercises.explain: The first option is full of vague buzzwords ('multifaceted strategic paradigm,' 'optimal placement methodologies') without saying anything concrete — a classic sign of AI padding. The other two give specific, actionable advice.

exercises.prompt: You're building a customer-support bot. The context window will contain: (1) a long product manual, (2) the user's specific complaint, (3) five past support tickets for background, (4) the exact refund policy the answer depends on. Where should the refund policy and the user's complaint go?

options.text: Bury them in the middle so the model has to 'work' for the answer, improving reasoning
Put the refund policy at the very start, and the user's complaint at the very end, right before asking the model to respond
Order everything alphabetically by document title for consistency
Put the refund policy and complaint in the middle, surrounded by the manual and tickets for context

exercises.explain: Because models attend most strongly to the start and end of the context, the two pieces the answer truly depends on — the refund policy (key fact) and the user's complaint (the actual question) — should sit at those high-attention edges. The manual and past tickets are supporting material, so they can safely occupy the 'lost-in-the-middle' zone. This positioning trick doesn't fix the model's attention bias, but it works around it by putting what matters where the model is most likely to notice it. This is the last lesson of this unit's core skills — great work mastering context rot and how to fight it!
